title: Merovingian script — a historical introduction
sources: E.A. Lowe, Codices Latini Antiquiores (1934 onwards) — scholarly catalogue of Latin manuscripts; PD for older volumes. Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain. General palaeographical sources on Merovingian script from late antique Frankish kingdom.
notes: READING article. Merovingian is the 6th-8th century Frankish book-script that evolved from late Roman cursive. Extremely difficult for modern readers: heavy ligatures, unusual letterform abbreviations, irregular height, letter e written as a high loop. Context: the Carolingian reform (Charlemagne, c.800) replaced Merovingian with the cleaner Carolingian minuscule; Merovingian is therefore the 'before' to Carolingian's 'after'. Cover: where Merovingian fits in the Latin script timeline, the key difficult letterforms (e, a, r, s), how to read a Merovingian manuscript (decoding strategy), its relationship to Uncial and Half-uncial. Modern revival: rarely attempted; useful background for calligraphers studying the full historical progression.
